sanskrit_devanagari:
सदृशं चेष्टते स्वस्याः प्रकृतेर्ज्ञानवानपि ।
प्रकृतिं यान्ति भूतानि निग्रहः किं करिष्यति ॥ ३३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
sadṛśaṁ ceṣṭate svasyāḥ
prakṛter jñānavān api |
prakṛtiṁ yānti bhūtāni
nigrahaḥ kiṁ kariṣyati || 33 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence as published)
exact (text body identical with vedabase; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.033 = MBh_06,025.033). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: Even a man of knowledge acts according to his own nature, for everyone follows the nature he has acquired from the three modes. What can repression accomplish?
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Unless one is situated on the transcendental platform of Kṛṣṇa consciousness, he cannot get free from the influence of the modes of material nature, as it is confirmed by the Lord in the Seventh Chapter (7.14).

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.33 Api, even; jnanavan, a man of wisdom-what to speak of a fool!; cestate, behaves; Sadrsam, according to;-what? svasyah, his own; prakrteh, nature. Nature means the impressions of virtue, vice, etc.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: sadṛśaṁ ceṣṭate svasyāḥ prakṛter jñānavān api — even the wise person acts according to his own prakṛti, the conditioning produced by prior karma and the gunas. prakṛtiṁ yānti bhūtāni — beings follow their nature. nigrahaḥ kiṁ kariṣyati — what will restraint, the surface-suppression of acts, accomplish? The verse names the insufficiency of mere willpower against prakṛti and sets up the diagnostic of 3.34.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.33 Such and such is the nature of the self, which is different from the Prakrti — this has to be always contemplated upon: thus declare the Sastras.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: even the jñānavān — the one who has knowledge — moves in accordance with his constitutional nature (prakṛti, formed by the gunas). All beings move thus. nigrahaḥ — restraint at the surface — does not change the underlying disposition. The remedy must operate at a different layer; 3.34 will name it (the seat of attachment-aversion in the sense-engagement, not in the senses themselves).

disagreements_among_translators.word: prakṛti (nature) / nigrahaḥ kiṁ kariṣyati (what will restraint do?)

positions.rendering: even a man of knowledge acts according to his own nature, for everyone follows the nature he has acquired from the three modes. What can repression accomplish?
even the wise act according to their natures, for all living beings are propelled by their natural tendencies. What will one accomplish by restraint?

prakṛti = the conditioning produced by previous karma and gunas, the saṁskāra-set the actor brings into this life; even the wise actor moves in accordance with this; pure suppression of the surface acts (nigrahaḥ) does not address the conditioning

prakṛti = the constitutional disposition formed by the gunas; all beings follow this disposition; mere restraint of the senses without the more fundamental cognitive shift (3.34) does not change anything

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both commentators agree that nigrahaḥ here means surface-level suppression of action without the underlying cognitive shift, and the verse names its insufficiency. Translators capture this but sometimes read prakṛti too narrowly as 'personality' or 'temperament.' The technical reading: prakṛti is the saṁskāra-conditioning of the gunas plus prior karma, deeper than what the actor experiences as 'character.'

literal_meaning: Even the wise person acts according to his own prakṛti (nature); beings follow their nature; what will restraint accomplish?
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna acknowledges the limit of mere willpower against prakṛti. Even the jñānavān (the wise one) sadṛśam ceṣṭate svasyāḥ prakṛteḥ (acts in accordance with his own nature). All beings follow their prakṛti; nigrahaḥ (restraint, suppression) accomplishes little. The verse sets up 3.34's diagnosis: attachment-and-aversion are seated in the engagement with sense-objects, not in the surface act of restraint.
